description: PTQ v2: quantization applied after training with no gradient updates. Scale S and zero-point Z derived from calibration data (static) or computed per-batch at runtime (dynamic). Three back-ends: dynamic (no calib), static FX sweep (4 observers), FX-graph with backend-tuned default qconfigs. Latency measured single-sample to match pruning v5. FLOPs computed via thop (graph unchanged — values equal baseline).

ptq_math.note: INT8 works well with PTQ; INT4 degrades because 15 levels gives large S → large |ε|, and errors accumulate through layers with no mechanism to correct them (that is QAT's job).

baseline.eval_source: re-evaluated inline (FIX-3) — not loaded from external JSON

results.description: Dynamic PTQ: only Linear weights quantized to qint8. S and Z computed on-the-fly per batch — no calibration data. Conv2d stays FP32. Fast to apply; no memory of x_min/x_max needed.
results.flops_note: PTQ (all backends) does not change the computation graph — MAC count is identical to the FP32 baseline. INT8 arithmetic yields ~2-4× throughput on CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI because 4 INT8 MACs fit in one VNNI instruction vs 1 FP32 FMA. GFLOPs reported here are dense FP32-equivalent MACs × 2; effective INT8 FLOPs from hardware counters would be ~4× lower.
results.inference_ms_note: Single-sample CPU latency (batch_size=1), matching pruning v5 convention. 200 timed passes after 20 warmup passes. CPU-only; no CUDA sync needed.

quantization_formulas.S: computed per-batch at runtime from weight range

results.description: Static PTQ (FX-graph, minmax): graph-traced fusion of Conv-BN-ReLU and residual adds. S and Z calibrated from 1024 training images. Weights and activations quantized to INT8.
results.flops_note: PTQ (all backends) does not change the computation graph — MAC count is identical to the FP32 baseline. INT8 arithmetic yields ~2-4× throughput on CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI because 4 INT8 MACs fit in one VNNI instruction vs 1 FP32 FMA. GFLOPs reported here are dense FP32-equivalent MACs × 2; effective INT8 FLOPs from hardware counters would be ~4× lower.
results.inference_ms_note: Single-sample CPU latency (batch_size=1), matching pruning v5 convention. 200 timed passes after 20 warmup passes. CPU-only; no CUDA sync needed.

quantization_formulas.max_err: |ε| ≤ S/2  →  smaller S = finer grid = less error

results.description: FX-graph static PTQ (backend=x86): torch.ao traces the computation graph, automatically fuses Conv-BN-ReLU and quantizes all eligible ops including residual adds. Same S/Z formulas as eager static; backend-tuned qconfig.
results.flops_note: PTQ (all backends) does not change the computation graph — MAC count is identical to the FP32 baseline. INT8 arithmetic yields ~2-4× throughput on CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI because 4 INT8 MACs fit in one VNNI instruction vs 1 FP32 FMA. GFLOPs reported here are dense FP32-equivalent MACs × 2; effective INT8 FLOPs from hardware counters would be ~4× lower.
results.inference_ms_note: Single-sample CPU latency (batch_size=1), matching pruning v5 convention. 200 timed passes after 20 warmup passes. CPU-only; no CUDA sync needed.

quantization_formulas.throughput: INT8 VNNI ~4× FP32 on AVX-512 CPUs

results.description: FX-graph static PTQ (backend=fbgemm): torch.ao traces the computation graph, automatically fuses Conv-BN-ReLU and quantizes all eligible ops including residual adds. Same S/Z formulas as eager static; backend-tuned qconfig.

quantization_formulas.throughput: INT8 VNNI ~4× FP32 on AVX-512 CPUs

fixes_from_v1.FIX_1_single_sample_latency: inference_ms now uses batch_size=1 (single-sample), matching pruning v5. v1 used batch_size=128 — that's batch throughput, not latency.
fixes_from_v1.FIX_2_flops: GFLOPs now computed per method via thop. PTQ does not change the graph — values match baseline. Explicitly stated in flops_note and flops_source per row.
fixes_from_v1.FIX_3_inline_baseline: Baseline accuracy re-evaluated on the same test loader in this script. v1 loaded accuracy from an external JSON, risking stale/mismatched values.
fixes_from_v1.FIX_4_deduplication: Quantized models returned from each runner and reused for saving. v1 re-ran all quantization passes from scratch in the saving block.

notes.dynamic: No calibration; Linear only; fastest to apply; Conv stays FP32
notes.static_fx: Calibration required; best INT8 accuracy; Conv-BN-ReLU auto-fused by FX
notes.fx_default: Backend-tuned qconfig; broadest op coverage; model must be torch.fx-traceable
notes.gpu_note: PyTorch static PTQ is CPU-only; GPU INT8 → TensorRT / ONNX Runtime
notes.speedup_note: INT8 ops ~2-4× faster than FP32 on CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI support
notes.vs_qat: PTQ requires no retraining and is fast; QAT closes the accuracy gap at INT4 by inserting fake-quant nodes during training and using the STE trick.
notes.vs_pruning: PTQ and mask-based pruning both leave the compute graph unchanged. PTQ reduces arithmetic precision (FP32→INT8); pruning introduces sparsity. PTQ achieves ~4× disk compression with near-zero accuracy drop. Pruning at 50% sparsity achieves ~2× compression only with sparse kernels. Comparison: flops_G should be equal across both; latency depends on hardware.
notes.disk_size_note: disk_size_mb uses torch.save(model) (full model, not state_dict) to ensure INT8 tensor packing is preserved in the serialized file.
notes.flops_note: PTQ (all backends) does not change the computation graph — MAC count is identical to the FP32 baseline. INT8 arithmetic yields ~2-4× throughput on CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI because 4 INT8 MACs fit in one VNNI instruction vs 1 FP32 FMA. GFLOPs reported here are dense FP32-equivalent MACs × 2; effective INT8 FLOPs from hardware counters would be ~4× lower.
